summary: I lived in Laos with my family before we had to escape in May 1979 during the planting season. Our country was falling apart, and people were leaving for Thailand. We left Laos with many relatives, about 50 to 100 people, carrying what we could and struggling especially with my young children. It was a difficult time, and we had to hide in caves and survive on roots and vegetation as we fled.

Though I never experienced it myself, my parents often told us about escaping from China. It was a story passed down through generations, and now I understand what they went through because we faced a similar fate. Our journey reminded me of those stories as we fled Laos, just like our elders fled China. It was a harsh and sad experience that connected me to my family's past.

After leaving Laos, we crossed into Thailand in May 1979 under the bright moonlight. Many others had already fled there, and we stayed in Thailand for about ten years. It was a place of refuge but not a permanent home, as we wondered whether to stay or move on to America. Eventually, we prepared ourselves and left Thailand on September 7th, spending a year in Phanat Nikhon before coming to the United States.

Before coming to the United States, we stayed in Phanat Nikhon for one year after leaving Thailand. It was a transitional place for us as we prepared for the next step in our journey. From there, we departed to the U.S. seeking a better life and opportunities for our children and grandchildren. Phanat Nikhon was an important stop on our long path to a new home.

We came to the United States for a better life and the chance to see our children and grandchildren grow up. When we arrived, we were too old to learn new skills or attend school, so we lived simply and endured hardships. Our children, however, were young and able to learn new things. The U.S. gave us hope and opportunity, even though we had to be patient and humble in this new land.
